cli

Class CommandFlagParser

  • java.lang.Object
    • cli.CommandFlagParser

  • public class CommandFlagParser
extends java.lang.Object
    This is the core access point for orchestrating the command-line parsing operations for integration in your Java console-based development projects.

    This class manages the lifecycle of command-line parsing through three distinct phases:

    1. Definition: Registering FlagRule objects using addDefinition(java.lang.String, cli.FlagType).
    2. Tokenisation: Breaking down raw input into manageable components.
    3. Execution: Processing tokens using polymorphic FlagHandler handlers.

    The engine follows POSIX conventions for short flags (e.g., -vh) and GNU conventions for long flags (e.g., --verbose). It supports clustered short flags, attached or "glued" values, and explicit value separators (=).

    Establish rules using addDefinition(java.lang.String, cli.FlagType) prior to parsing. Constraints are defined by FlagType:

    Rule Definitions
    Rule Description
    ARG_BLANK Boolean switch. No value permitted.
    ARG_REQUIRED Mandatory flag. Requires an associated value.
    ARG_OPTIONAL Optional flag. Accepts a value if provided.
    SEP_REQUIRED Value MUST be joined by an equals separator (e.g., --flag=val).
    SEP_OPTIONAL Value MAY be joined by an equals separator.

    Note that the SEP rules also recognise comma-separated arguments, allowing the parser to construct a list of values for retrieval (e.g., --value=12,24,36).

    Examples of supported syntax:

    • cmd -g vulfeed.dat
    • cmd --csv
    • cmd -path /var/log/value.log
    • cmd --depth=7
    • cmd -b=3
    • cmd -ofile.txt (Concatenated/Attached: -o is the option, file.txt is the argument)
    • cmd -hv (Clustered: Two standalone short options)

    The parser correctly identifies complex concatenated tokens, such as -d787 (where -d is the flag and 787 is the value) or -avb=747 (where -a and -v are boolean switches and -b is assigned the value 747).

    Since:
    28 February 2026
    Version:
    2.0
    Author:
    Trevor Maggs

      All Methods Static Methods Instance Methods Concrete Methods 
      Modifier and Type Method Description
      void addDefinition(java.lang.String name, FlagType type)
      Registers a new flag rule.
      boolean existsFlag(java.lang.String name)
      Determines if the specified flag or option has been processed and is available for value retrieval.
      java.lang.String getFirstFreeArgument()
      Retrieves the first stand-alone argument from the value set.
      FlagRule getFlagRule(java.lang.String name)
      Retrieves a flag rule if registered.
      java.lang.String getFreeArgumentByIndex(int k)
      Retrieves the free-standing argument from a specific position in the value set.
      int getFreeArgumentCount()
      Returns the count of free-standing arguments not associated with any registered flags.
      java.lang.String getValueByFlag(java.lang.String name)
      Retrieves the value of the specified flag if present.
      java.lang.String getValueByFlag(java.lang.String name, int index)
      Retrieves the value of the specified flag by its index in the value set.
      int getValueLength(java.lang.String name)
      Returns the size of the value set which the specified flag is associated with.
      static boolean isLongOption(java.lang.String token)
      Queries whether the specified token represents a long flag starting with double leading dashes (--), following the GNU convention.
      static boolean isNegativeNumber(java.lang.String token)
      Determines if the specified token represents a real negative numeric value.
      static boolean isOption(java.lang.String token)
      Verifies the specified token represents a valid command flag or option.
      static boolean isShortOption(java.lang.String token)
      Queries whether the specified token represents a valid short flag or option, following the POSIX convention.
      void parse()
      Executes the parsing logic against the raw arguments provided at construction.
      private void processRangeValues(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String value)
      Collapses a comma-separated token into manageable individual values.
      private void processSingleValue(java.lang.String name, java.lang.String value)
      Processes the specified value associated with the flag being processed, if available.

      Enables or disables debugging mode.
      void setFreeArgumentLimit(int count)
      Sets the maximum number of arguments (operands) permitted.

      Strips leading dashes from a flag token.
      private void validateOperands()
      Validates the integrity and quantity of captured operands.
      private void validateRuleConstraints()
      Validates the current state of all rules against their defined FlagType constraints.

    • Constructor Detail

      • CommandFlagParser

        public CommandFlagParser(java.lang.String[] args)
        Initialises the parser with raw command-line arguments.
        Parameters:
        args - the command-line arguments
        Throws:
        java.lang.IllegalArgumentException - if args is null or empty

    • Method Detail

      • stripLeadingDashes

        public static java.lang.String stripLeadingDashes(java.lang.String token)
        Strips leading dashes from a flag token.
        Parameters:
        token - the raw token, for example: "-v" or "--verbose"
        Returns:
        the token content without leading '-' or '--'

      • isNegativeNumber

        public static boolean isNegativeNumber(java.lang.String token)
        Determines if the specified token represents a real negative numeric value.

        A real negative number must start with a single dash ('-') followed immediately by one or more digits, with an optional single decimal point (e.g., "-5.7", "-0.5").

        This method rejects non-numeric strings ("-a"), malformed numbers ("-2.2.2"), standalone dashes ("-", "--"), and positive numbers.

        Parameters:
        token - the raw command-line token to evaluate
        Returns:
        true if the token matches the negative numeric pattern

      • isOption

        public static boolean isOption(java.lang.String token)
        Verifies the specified token represents a valid command flag or option.
        Parameters:
        token - the entry extracted from the command line
        Returns:
        true if the token is identified as a valid flag or option

      • isLongOption

        public static boolean isLongOption(java.lang.String token)
        Queries whether the specified token represents a long flag starting with double leading dashes (--), following the GNU convention.

        This flag can optionally include an appended value, such as --flag[value] or --flag=value.

        Parameters:
        token - the command-line token to evaluate
        Returns:
        true if the token follows the GNU long-option format

      • isShortOption

        public static boolean isShortOption(java.lang.String token)
        Queries whether the specified token represents a valid short flag or option, following the POSIX convention.

        A short flag consists of a single character preceded by a single leading dash (e.g., -S). Also supports clustered flags and appended values, such as -SV, -S=V, -S1S2V, or -S1S2=V1,V2,V3.

        Parameters:
        token - the raw command-line token to evaluate
        Returns:
        true if the token follows the POSIX short-option format

      • setDebug

        public void setDebug(boolean mode)
        Enables or disables debugging mode.

        This setting is propagated to downstream components to adjust their diagnostic output and behavioural responses accordingly.

        Parameters:
        mode - true to enable debugging, or false to disable (default)

      • addDefinition

        public void addDefinition(java.lang.String name,
                          FlagType type)
                   throws java.text.ParseException
        Registers a new flag rule.
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier. Valid dashes (e.g., "-v" or "--portal") must be provided
        type - the FlagType defining how arguments and separators are handled
        Throws:
        java.text.ParseException - if the flag is already registered

      • getFlagRule

        public FlagRule getFlagRule(java.lang.String name)
        Retrieves a flag rule if registered. Use it if value extraction is required.
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier
        Returns:
        the FlagRule if matched, or null if no such rule exists

      • setFreeArgumentLimit

        public void setFreeArgumentLimit(int count)
        Sets the maximum number of arguments (operands) permitted. These are free-standing arguments not associated with any specific flag or option. There is a maximum number of operands applied to it.
        Parameters:
        count - the number of operands allowed. If exceeded during parsing, a ParseException will be thrown. Default is one argument allowed if not defined
        Throws:
        java.lang.IllegalArgumentException - if the specified count is out of bounds

      • existsFlag

        public boolean existsFlag(java.lang.String name)
        Determines if the specified flag or option has been processed and is available for value retrieval.

        As an example, the following snippet checks if a particular -f option has been set. 

         
 if (cli.existsFlag("-f"))
 {
     // Do something here
 }
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier with or without leading dashes
        Returns:
        true if the flag exists or false if the flag is unrecognised

      • getValueByFlag

        public java.lang.String getValueByFlag(java.lang.String name)
        Retrieves the value of the specified flag if present.

        Example: the following snippet retrieves the value of the -f flag. 

         
   System.out.println(cli.getValueByFlag("-f"));
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier with or without leading dashes
        Returns:
        the value being requested or a blank string if none

      • getValueByFlag

        public java.lang.String getValueByFlag(java.lang.String name,
                                       int index)
                                throws java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException
        Retrieves the value of the specified flag by its index in the value set.
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier with or without leading dashes
        index - the position of where the value is located in a collection
        Returns:
        the value of the specified flag or a blank string if there is no value
        Throws:
        java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException - if the index is out of bounds

      • getValueLength

        public int getValueLength(java.lang.String name)
        Returns the size of the value set which the specified flag is associated with.
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier with or without leading dashes
        Returns:
        the size of the values held by the processed flag, otherwise zero if none

      • getFreeArgumentCount

        public int getFreeArgumentCount()
        Returns the count of free-standing arguments not associated with any registered flags.
        Returns:
        the number of free arguments (operands)

      • getFirstFreeArgument

        public java.lang.String getFirstFreeArgument()
        Retrieves the first stand-alone argument from the value set.
        Returns:
        the first stand-alone argument, or an empty string if none exists

      • getFreeArgumentByIndex

        public java.lang.String getFreeArgumentByIndex(int k)
        Retrieves the free-standing argument from a specific position in the value set.
        Parameters:
        k - the zero-based index within the argument list
        Returns:
        the stand-alone argument at the specified position, or an empty string if the index is out of bounds

      • parse

        public void parse()
           throws java.text.ParseException
        Executes the parsing logic against the raw arguments provided at construction.

        This method performs a single-pass parsing operation. It identifies flags, routes to required handlers, collects operands, and finally validates the following constraints.

        • The free-standing argument limit (see setFreeArgumentLimit(int)).
        • The presence of all mandatory flags.
        Throws:
        java.text.ParseException - if an unrecognised flag is encountered, any required argument is missing, or validation cannot be completed

      • processRangeValues

        private void processRangeValues(java.lang.String name,
                                java.lang.String value)
                         throws java.text.ParseException
        Collapses a comma-separated token into manageable individual values.

        Each element is trimmed of leading and trailing whitespace. Empty elements (e.g., "val1,,val2") are ignored. Validated values are processed via processSingleValue(java.lang.String, java.lang.String).

        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier
        value - the raw string containing potential multiple values
        Throws:
        java.text.ParseException - if the flag associated with the name is unknown or does not accept arguments

      • processSingleValue

        private void processSingleValue(java.lang.String name,
                                java.lang.String value)
                         throws java.text.ParseException
        Processes the specified value associated with the flag being processed, if available.
        Parameters:
        name - the flag identifier
        value - the single data associated with the flag. A value of null is accepted if the value is not available
        Throws:
        java.text.ParseException - if the flag associated with the name is unknown or does not accept arguments

      • validateOperands

        private void validateOperands()
        Validates the integrity and quantity of captured operands.

        This method ensures that no unrecognised flags, prefixed with a dash ('-'), were mistakenly captured as operands and verifies that the total number of free-standing arguments does not exceed the defined limit.

      • validateRuleConstraints

        private void validateRuleConstraints()
                              throws java.text.ParseException
        Validates the current state of all rules against their defined FlagType constraints.

        This method performs a comprehensive integrity check:

        • All mandatory flags have been identified in the input stream.
        • Separator enforcement rules (required vs. forbidden) are strictly followed.
        • Flags requiring an argument have at least one value assigned.
        Throws:
        java.text.ParseException - if any flag violates its structural or presence constraints. The exception message aggregates all identified errors
